coredata.dc:title: Ultrafast responsive humidity sensor based on roasted gram derived carbon quantum dots: Experimental and theoretical study
coredata.dc:creator: Chaudhary, Priyanka
Maurya, Dheeraj Kumar
Yadav, Sarita
Pandey, Anwesh
Tripathi, Ravi Kant
Yadav, B.C.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we have reported the sustainable, cost-effective and rapid synthesis of gram derived carbon quantum dots (GCQD) employed as capacitive humidity sensor. Synthesis of GCQDs C-1 & C-2 at different carbonization temperature has been performed by pyrolysis method. Crystallinity of as prepared GCQDs were confirmed by XRD and TEM analysis revealing the average particle size 5.5 and 2 nm for C-1 & C-2 respectively. Optical properties of samples were further analysed by UV–vis and photoluminescence meter which gave the band gap as 3.82 and 4.20 eV. Compositional analysis of GCQDs were confirmed by using XPS. GCQDs were subjected to sensing test at frequency 20 – 2 MHz. The C-2 gave an enhanced sensing performance with an average sensitivitiy of 254.861 pF/%RH. Also, sensor showed rapid response and recovery times of 7.3 & 4.7 s respectively. Theoretical modelling has been performed to further look into the insight of interaction between functional group of GCQDs and water vapour molecules. Transient parameters such as charge density, HOMO-LUMO gap, electron clouds and H-bonds were calculated using B3LYP hybrid functional at 6−31 g diffused and polarised basis set. This study provides an insight chemistry of GCQDs with water toward an efficient humidity sensor development.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Pyrolysis synthesis
Electrical humidity sensing
Molecular modelling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Interaction of GCQD with water molecule (H2O)
Electrostatic and hydrogen bonding charge densities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Priyanka Chaudhary acknowledges UGC, New Delhi for non NET fellowship. S. Yadav acknowledges DST for Inspire Faculty research grant ( DST/INSPIRE/04/2016/001600 ). Dr. Ravi Kant Tripathi acknowledges to the Government of India for providing research grant as UGC sponsored Dr. D.S. Kothari Post-doctoral fellowship (No. F.4-2/2006(BSR)/PH/16-17/0054 ). Authors would also like to thank Prof. Devesh Kumar for Gaussian 09 software package and valuable discussion in theoretical part.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of preparation of GCQDs using pyrolysis technique. (For interpretation of the references to colour in the figure text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) FTIR spectra of GCQDs (b) XRD pattern (c) Oxidation state and (d) Binding energies curve of C-2.

(a) TEM image of C-1 at 100 nm scale (b) HRTEM image of the GCQDs at 1 nm scale (c) Particle size distribution curve of C-1 (d) TEM image of C-2 at 20 nm (e) HRTEM image of GCQDs at 5 nm scale (f) Particle size distribution curve for the sample C-2.

UV absorption spectra of GCQDs (a) Absorbance Vs wavelength (nm) plot for the C-1 with an energy band gap of 3.82 eV (b) Absorbance Vs wavelength (nm) for the C-2 with an energy band gap of 4.2 eV. (c) Photoluminscence of C-1 with an emission peak at 454 nm (d) an emission peak of C-2 at 428 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in the figure text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Raman spectra of C-1 and (b) C-2 samples.

Sensing characteristics of GCQDs based sensor at different operating frquencies (20 Hz -2 MHz) (a,b) increasing and decreasing mode curve at different frequencies of C-1 (c,d) increasing and decreasing mode at different frequencies of C-2.
(a) Adsorption and desorption curve with hysteresis of gram carbon quantum dot (a) C-1 and (b) C-2. Inset shows the corresponding normalized sensor response curve.
Variation of capacitance with respect to frequencies (a) increasing mode (b) decreasing mode of C-1 (c) increasing mode (d) decreasing mode curve of C-2.
Response and recovery times curve of C-1 and C-2.
Variation of capacitance with time (a) stability curve of C-1 (b) stabilitity curve of C-2.
Schematic of humidity sensing mechanism of GCQDs.
Formation of H-bond interaction for (a) one, (b) two and (c) three water molecules with GCQDs.

H-donor/acceptor clouded regions of (a) one, (b) two and (c) three water molecules with GCQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in the figure text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The electron cloud densities in interaction of (a) one, (b) two and (c) three water molecules with GCQD.
HOMO-LUMO molecular orbital structure of gram carbon quantum dots.
The literature survey related to capacitive carbon based humidity sensor.

head.ce:title: Ultrafast responsive humidity sensor based on roasted gram derived carbon quantum dots: Experimental and theoretical study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanomaterials and Sensors Research Laboratory, Department of Physics, Babasaheb Bhimrao Ambedkar University, Lucknow, 226025, U.P., India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanomaterials and Sensors Research Laboratory, Department of Physics, Babasaheb Bhimrao Ambedkar University, Lucknow-226025, U.P., India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electro-materials Research Laboratory, Centre for Nanoscience and Technology, Pondicherry University, Puducherry, 605014, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Materials Research Laboratory, Department of Physics, Babasaheb Bhimrao Ambedkar University, Lucknow, 226025, U.P., India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Materials Research Laboratory, Department of Physics, Babasaheb Bhimrao Ambedkar University, Lucknow-226025, U.P., India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we have reported the sustainable, cost-effective and rapid synthesis of gram derived carbon quantum dots (GCQD) employed as capacitive humidity sensor. Synthesis of GCQDs C-1 & C-2 at different carbonization temperature has been performed by pyrolysis method. Crystallinity of as prepared GCQDs were confirmed by XRD and TEM analysis revealing the average particle size 5.5 and 2 nm for C-1 & C-2 respectively. Optical properties of samples were further analysed by UV–vis and photoluminescence meter which gave the band gap as 3.82 and 4.20 eV. Compositional analysis of GCQDs were confirmed by using XPS. GCQDs were subjected to sensing test at frequency 20 – 2 MHz. The C-2 gave an enhanced sensing performance with an average sensitivitiy of 254.861 pF/%RH. Also, sensor showed rapid response and recovery times of 7.3 & 4.7 s respectively. Theoretical modelling has been performed to further look into the insight of interaction between functional group of GCQDs and water vapour molecules. Transient parameters such as charge density, HOMO-LUMO gap, electron clouds and H-bonds were calculated using B3LYP hybrid functional at 6−31 g diffused and polarised basis set. This study provides an insight chemistry of GCQDs with water toward an efficient humidity sensor development.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are quasi-spherical shaped structures in the size less than 10 nm. CQDs are mainly self-possessed of sp2 carbon containing nitrogen, oxygen and newly doped heteroatoms. It emerges as a versatile carboneous nanomaterial due to its unique chemical, physical and biological features [1,2]. Carbon quantum dot was accidentally discovered by Xu et al. in 2004 [3]. They observed the exceptional fluoroscence properties while developing the single walled carbon nanotube (SWCNTs). Later on, further studies were conducted on synthesis of carbon quantum dots with improved photoluminescence (PL) characteristics. CQDs due to its high aqueous solubility and strong luminescent characteristics, used in nanolights [3]. Photoluminsence of CQDs are highly efficient for application in bioimaging/ drug delivery and biosensors development [4]. CQDs acts as an electron acceptors and donors as a result of its excellent electronic properties [5,6].

CQDs are generally synthesized by two approches: bottom-up and top-down; in bottom up method, organic molecules like folic acid, gelation and citric acid has been used as preliminary material to comprehensive in carbon quantum dots [6,7]. In top-down approach, the petroleum coke prepared by oxidation process with H2SO4 and KMnO4 has been used to synthesize CQDs [8]. Both techniques are quite hazardous for human as well as environment due to involvement of toxic solvents [9]. To overcome the above said drawbacks of the previously reported synthesis techniques of CQDs, researchers have put a significant effort towards the preparation of CQDs without using any organic compounds, particularly termed as green synthesis [10–14]. Lu W. et al. have reported the formation of carbon quantum dots via green synthesis method for Hg2+ detection [15]. This group also investigated in the field of sensitive detection of metal oxides by using the microwave assisted green synthesis route and developed the fluorescent carbon quantum dots from wheat flour [16]. Zhou J et al. reported the green synthesis of CQDs from watermelon peel for their application in cell imaging probes [17]. In 2013 Ramanan V et al. used green synthesis for prepration of fluorescent carbon quantum dots [18]. One step synthesis method and hydrothermal method using orange juice [19] were used for the synthesis of CQDs. Liu W et al. in 2017 prepared carbon dots derived from rose-heart radish via green synthesis for Fe3+ detection and cell imaging, Pandey S et al. in 2013 synthesised CQDs at room temperature [20–22]. In arrears to the promising properties, CQDs have been widely used in applications as, optical and electrical [23], solar cell [24], photocatalyst [25], bioimaging [26] and humidity sensors [27]. Carbon based materials are of immense interest in humidity sensing due to their exceptional physical, chemical and electrical properties. Humidity plays a vital role in health monitoring, manufacturing, food packaging, and testing processes [28]. A lot of research work has been reported for carboneous material towards humidity sensing application [29,30]. Recently, it has been reported that ∼80 % of the humidity sensors market is dominated by capacitive type humidity sensor. Such types of humidity sensors accompany many advantages like low power intake and high output signals as compared to other humidity sensors [31]. These sensors are capable of measuring the humidity level via induced charge in dielectric constant of hygroscopic layers. Capacitive based humidity sensors are the combination of two interdigitated electrodes (IDE) enclosed by a dielectric layer, which is responsible for humidity changes. Humidity sensor is very important for human comfort and environment, due to the increasing demand in industry, agriculture and daily life [32].

For the sensor point of view the surface morphology is very important for validating any sensing material. High sensitivity requires the availability of surface fuctional group and wide optical band gap of the sensing material. Being motivated by these accomplishments, we have reported a green synthesis method for synthesizing highly flouroscent carbon quantum dots. It is synthesised by modified pyrolysis [33] of gram peels and further tested as an humidity sensing material. Carbon quantum dots have unique charatceristics like presence of large oxygen-contained functional group on surface and large surface to volume ratio responsible for high humidity sensing. Humidity sensing can be possible because of the adsorption of water molecules and formation of hydrogen bond in CQDs through the surface oxygen-contained groups facilitating electron movement among the water molecules and carbon quantum dots.

The novelty of this work relies on the facile, cost-effective and eco-friendly synthesis of fluoroscent GCQDs derived from waste peel of gram with its application in electrical humidity sensor in a sustainable, cost effective and nontoxic approach. GCQDs show the higher sensitivity comparable to other metal oxides and metallopolymeric materials reported so far. Modern sensors are facing the problem of large response and recovery times. In this regards, improved sensing characteristics was achieved from the CQDs derived from waste peel of easily available gram. Also, we have performed a theoretical study regarding the interaction of water molecule on GCQDs surface using DFT based B3LYP hybrid functional at 6−31 g diffused and polarised basis set. Theoretical results were found in the close agreement with experimental data that obtained from humidity sensing by GCQDs. The literature survey related to capacitive carbon based humidity sensor has been carried out and tabulated as Table 1 which founds the significance of carbon based humidity sensor.

The synthesis of gram peel derived carbon quantum dots (GCQD) was performed by modified pyrolysis method [33]. Initially, roasted grams were purchased from local market and subsequently peeled off to get the peels. As collected gram peels were kept in a crucible and further subjected to the carbonization procces at two diferent temperatures 200 °C and 450 °C for 8 h in a digital electric furnace (METRIX). The roasted gram peels were pyorlysed at 200 °C and 450 °C, coded as C-1 and C-2 respectively. The brownish coloured gram peel turned into dark black after carbonization process. The dark black shells were finely grounded with mortar pestle to obtain a fine black powder. Thus 0.001 g of the fine powder of each sample C-1 and C-2 were dissolved in 15 mL DI water and stirred for 15 h to get a homogeneous and transparent solution. This solution was filtered through 0.22 μm pore filter paper and filterate was collected with an appearance of light brown color solution. Formation of CQDs were physically confirmed by illuminating both samples under UV radiation. C-1 showed the dark sky blue color, whereas C-2 exhibited the light blue color. The schematic for synthesis of CQDs is depicted in Fig. 1 .

The structure and average size distribution of GCQDs were analysed by Transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL-2100 F). Optical properties of GCQDs were characterized using photolumisence spectroscopy (Spectroflouorimeter-Flouorolog–FL3-11). UV absorption studies were performed using UV–vis spectrophotometer (Evolution 201) in the wavelength range from 190 to 1100 nm. XPS was carried out using the instrument (PHI 5000 Versa Probe II, FEI Inc. spectrometer), revealing the chemical compositon of as synthesized GCQDs. The Raman analysis was carried out using the instrument (UniRAM confocal micro Raman mapping system).

During the theoretical study, the distances between water molecules and GCQD structures were kept as close as 3 Å and then the whole system was put under geometry optimization using B3LYP hybrid functional at 6−31 g diffused and polarised basis set of Gaussian 09 software package.

The FTIR spectra of GCQDs (C-1 & C-2) is shown in Fig. 2 (a). The broad peaks of the hydroxyl group are found in the range (3300 cm−1–3400 cm−1) [41], revealing the presence of vibrational stretching and hydrogen bonding. It can be observed that the intensity of the hydroxyl bond of C-1 is lesser intensity than C-2, resulting the larger number of OH− groups present on the surface of GCQDs (C-2). Some other groups such as NH stretching signifying the conjugation of amine groups, sp3 hybridazation, C–H bonds were also confirmed the stability of GCQDs. The additional groups CO carboxyl bond represent the more oxygen and carbon bonding, NH bending and Alkene group (CC) are observed in C-2. From the results, we can conclude that the C-2 is better GCQDs as compared to C-1 and may enchance the properties of the GCQDs.

The XRD patterns of the GCQDs (C-1 & C-2) are shown in Fig. 2(b). The lattice spacing values are different in both GCQDs. The C-1 shows lattice spacing of graphene (002) [42] while C-2 shows the two peaks at (002) and (101). Both samples reveal an intense characteristic peak with a position corresponds to interplanar spacing of ∼ 3.77 Å, particularly the distance between the (002) planes of bulk graphite (3.34 Å). Therefore, GCQDs have a carbon structure [43].

Fig. 2(c & d) shows the elemental analysis of gram CQDs of sample C-2. The XPS graphs of the optimized C-2, confirms the existence of carbon and oxygen in synthesized as quantum dots. Deconvulated peaks in XPS at the positions 285.8 eV, 285 eV and 287 eV correspond to available bonds between CC, CO and CO respectively [44]. The pronounced peak at the 285.8 eV reveals the sp2 graphitic structure of as synthesized GCQD. No traces of impurities were found in the XPS analysis which confirms the purity of as synthesized GCQD.

The particle size distribution of GCQDs can be seen through TEM images of C-1 and C-2 which are well shown in Fig. 3 (a–f). TEM image of C-1 shown in Fig. 3(a) exhibits the uniform size distribution of particles. Histogram plot of particle size ranges 2−10 nm and the most of the particles have average size 5.5 nm as shown in Fig. 3(c). From Fig. 3(d–f) the TEM images of the C-2 confirmed the uniform particle size distribution of the gram quantum dots at 20 nm scale. From Fig. 3(f), it is confirmed that size of the particles lies in the range 2−8 nm. Minor particle size is a peculiar demand for sensor application owing to the reason that as size of the particle decreases and the surface to volume ratio increases. Herein, it can be concluded that both samples C-1 and C-2 are suitable for humidity sensing application.

Fig. 4 (a) shows that the absoption spectra of the C-1 and Tauc plot in inset revealing a energy band gap of 3.82 eV. Fig. 4(b) shows the absorption spectra of C-2 which corresponds the 4.2 eV band gap. These absoption peaks also give the information that synthesized material is of direct band gap type. The characteristic energy band gap was calculated using the formula reported in the Manuscript by Sarita et al. [29]. The conclsion of UV–vis absorption displays that two peaks exist with and optical band gaps 3.82 eV and 4.2 eV, which might be allotted to π-π* electronic transitions.

Photoluminscence is the prominent characteristics shown by the quantum dots. It is the optical emission spectra which shows the size-dependent properties and also characteristic symbol of quantum confinement. The GCQDs shows two emission spectra at 454 nm for sample C-1 and second is 428 nm for sample C-2 respectively with an excited wavelength (λex) of 360 nm. The aqueous solution showing two colors in UV light chamber placed as inset image in Fig. 4(c,d). Therefore, on varying the excitation wavelength the gradual shift in emission is obvious. Hence we can say thatthe PL mechanism is depended on the size and surface of the quantum dots. Also, transition spectra are dependent on excitation wavelength. PL spectra exhibits blue shift among quantum dots. This may be due to the enhanced surface area present in GCQDs [45]. Thus C-2 shows the better luminescence property as compared to C-1.

The Raman analysis of carbon quantum dot (C-1 and C-2) are shown in Fig. 5 (a & b). Ranam peaks confirmed the presence of bond stetching carbon quantum dots. From Fig. 5(a) the positions of bands (D & G) for sample C-1 are 1340 cm−1 and 1358 cm−1 and for sample C-2, these D and G are 1549 cm−1 and 1587 cm−1 respectively. The concentration of the D-band is relative to the amount of disordered sp2 carbon and for the G-band it is proportional to the amount of ordered graphitic sp2 carbon enclosed in the evaluated samples. The determination of graphitization degree confirmed by intensity ratio of ID/IG. According to this ratio, low value means that the graphitization degree is high and the conductivity of the material is also increased [46]. The intensity ratio calculated for C-1 and C-2 GCQDs, C-1 gives the ID/IG which is 0.59 and for C-2 it is 0.32 respectively, which are shown in Fig. 5(a & b). From the Raman analysis, it can be concluded that the C-2 shows the lowest intensity ratio with high conducitivity [47].

The capacitive humidity sensing characteristics of Gram carbon quantum dots based fabricated film deposited on the borosilicate glass. The spin coating method was used for the fabrication of sensing element. Humidity sensing is mainly a surface phenomenon that depends on the adsorption and desorption of water molecules. The sensing set-up of the electrical humidity sensing already mentioned in our previous paper [30] and in present work shows in graphical abstract. Initially the fabricated film was dried and to take the increasing mode sensing data inside the chamber, the potassium sulphate solution was put in the chamber. This process called as humidification. The decreasing mode data was analysed by dehumidification inside the chamber by saturated aqueous solution of potassium hydroxide. The variations in capacitance corresponding with relative humidity (%RH) of the sensing film were noted by Impedance Analyser (Wayne Kerr Precession Component Analyser, 6440B), at operating temperature 300 K.

The sensing characteristics of fabricated humidity sensor was tested in the frequency range of (20 Hz-2 MHz) at ambient temperature. Fig. 6 (a & b) shows the sensing characteristics of C-1 in increasing mode i.e. 10–95 %RH and decreasing mode i.e. 95 – 10 %RH. Similar studies were also conducted for C-2 presented in Fig. 6(c & d). It can be perceived from the sensing curves that capacitance linearly increases with relative humidity at lower frequency for both C-1 and C-2. From the humidity sensing curve, the capacitance increases with the relative humidity in increasing mode and decreases with decreasing in relative humidity.

The sensitivity of the C-1 and C-2 was measured at different frequencies 20 Hz - 2 MHz, mentioned in Tables 2 and 3
. At a frequency of 20 Hz, the highest sensitivity was found for both samples. The primary reasons to it is the slight change in the direction of electrical pitch at small frequency which facilitates the space charge separation with the adsorbed water molecule. When subjected to high frequency, the direction of electrical field changes quickly, and the separation of the adsorbed water molecules could not latch up [48]. Owing to this, the dielectric persistent is minor and free with respect to relative humidity. Therefore, the higher sensitivities were found for both samples at low frequency.

The maximum value of capacitance remained found at 95 %RH at 20 Hz and the lowest capacitance value found at 10 %RH at 2 MHz for C-1 and C-2 in increasing mode. The increasing and decreasing mode shows the capacitance tends to increase from 45 %RH to 95 %RH. The sensing curve grew exponential with respect to the comparative humidity. Similarly, in decreasing mode the sensor exponentially decreases corresponding to the relative humidity. The concerned reason for higher sensitivity of C-2 is devoted to lesser particle size (∼ 4–5) nm of the GCQDs. This is due to enhanced surface area responsible for the increase in the sensitivity of the quantum dots. Sensitivity can be calculated from following equation:
(1)
S e n s i t i v i t y =
C
f i n a l
-
C
i n i t i a l
% R H
f i n a l
-
% R H
i n i t i a l
Where
C
f i n a l
and
C
i n i t i a l
are the capacitive values for C-1 and C-2 samples at final and initial %RH levels, respectively.

Hysteresis is a crucial drawback for capacitive humidity sensors. It may cause distortion of the sensing material owing to water groups and hence, influences the sensor performance. The nature of hysteresis curve followed by the sensing depends on the successive variation of relative humidity and capacitance value [49]. Hysteresis was calculated for both samples (C-1 and C-2) using following equation [50]:
(2)
C
d e s o r p t i o n -
C
a d s o r p t i o n
S e n s i t i v i t y
( %
R H )
where C is capacitance value measured at different %RH during the adsorption and desorption mode. According to the calculation the maximum hysteresis were observed at 95 %RH respectively in both samples (C-1 and C-2). The maximum value of hysteresis for C-1 is 22.121 %RH with average value of 1.934 %RH and for C-2, the maximum value of hysteresis is 3.945 %RH with average value of 1.034 %RH.

The sensor response was calculated from the range 15 %RH to 95 %RH for both samples C-1 and C-2 at 20 Hz frequency. From the Fig. 7 (a–d) we can see that the normalized response Y displays an exponential fitting of the %RH X, the corresponding function also has been depicted as inset of Fig. 7.

The graph between capacitance and frequency at dissimilar relative humidity values are presented in Fig. 8 (a–d). At higher frequency, the capacitance is negligible and independent of the humid atmosphere. From curves, it can be concluded that the capacitance values are maximum at lower frequency and minimum at higher frequency. The capacitance expontential change corresponding with the relative humidity. At 20 Hz the values of capacitance varies from 2.64 pF to 8751 pF for C-1 and 5.28 pF–10502 pF for C-2. The higher sensitivity was found for C-2 rather than C-1 at 20 Hz.

Rapid sensor response and recovery times are eseential factors for any good sensor [51]. Fig. 9 (a & b) displays the response and recovery curve where humidity varies from 10 %RH to 95 %RH. The response and recovery were estimated by exponential fitting for C-1 and C-2 respectively. For C-1 the response and recovery times were found as 13.3 s and 14.1 s respectively. Similarly for C-2 the response and recovery times were found 7.3 & 4.7 s respectively.

The stability of the sensing element was observed at optimum frequency 20 Hz. The curve was tested at various humidity range (15, 35, 55, 75 and 95 %RH) [52] for both C-1 and C-2. The reliability of sensor for a long term process depends on the stability of the material. A good sensor should have high sensitivity, short response, recovery time, and long-time durability. From Fig. 10 (a, b) we can say that at 95 %RH there is no variation in capacitance of GCQDs based humidity sensor with time and thus results are more stable. Also, the changes in capacitance at other humidity levels are very less, so GCQDs based sensor shows a good stability at optimum frequency.

The humidity sensing mechanism of carbon based materials (CNTs, Graphene oxide and Carbon quantum dots) depends on the existence of hydrophilic functional groups present on its surface such as hydroxyl groups. As we have already explained in the synthesis method that GCQDs were dissolved in deionized water and no other chemical reagents were used. So GCQDs do not have any type of impurity in the solution phase. As synthesized GCQDs have oxygen-contained groups such as COOH and OH groups on the surface which show an significant role in humidity sensing mechanism [53]. The capacitive based humidity sensor performs according to the change in capacitance comparative to the change of relative humidity (%RH). For the capacitive humidity sensor, the sensing mechanism depends on the active layer of dielectric constant and also the collection of water molecules of the surface in sensing material. Since, water is a polar molecule, so change in dielectric constant is an essential parameter for the assessment of performance of sensor.

For the capacitive humidity sensor the association between the relative humidity and capacitance may be explained by the given equation;
(3)
C = ( ε
r
- i
γ
ω
ε 0
)
C 0
Where C shows the capacitance of the sensing element and ω denotes frequency. From above equation, it is obvious that the capacitance is directly proportional to C0 whereas, it is inversely proportional to frequency ω [eq. 3].
ε 0
is permittivity of free space and the value of dielectric permittivity of pure water molecule is 78.54. For the humidity sensor, the hygroscopic nature of material is essential. Also, working of capacitance based humidity sensor depends on the polarization effect due to dielectric nature of the material and distance between the electrode areas.

The sensing mechanism may also be explained by Grotthuss chain reaction. Initially, the sensing component is free from water molecules and when exposed the moisture, surface of sensing element adsorbs water vapour in a larger amount. Consequently, there is an change of dielectric constant value of the sensing film. The water molecule breaks into two parts H+ and OH− ions. OH− ions interact with metal ions to form the chemisorbed layer and hence, small change in capacitance value was observed which is well shown in Fig. 11 (a). In mid humidity region, the protons (H+) increase the conductivity of the material and forms the physiosorption layer. Due to the formation of layer, value of capacitance sharply changed which can be easily observed in Fig. 11(b). In high humidity region, the capacitance value rise exponentially due to multi physiosorbed layer formation on the sensing element. The water molecules are singly bonded in physiosorption layer so that these can be easily polarized and due to this phenomenon the capacitance increases. Next from the Fig. 11(c) the protons have freedom to move inside the layer so one more layer of physorbed layer formed, which is known as second physiosorbed layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of GCQD with water molecule (H2O)
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dot structures were gradually aligned with one, two, three water molecule, etc. The distances between water molecules and CQD structures were kept as close as 3 Å for keen interception of their mutual interactions and then the whole system was put under geometry minimization using DFT based hybrid functional, B3LYP using 6−31 g** basis set of Gaussian 09 software package [54]. Here, the first (*) corresponds to diffused functions whereas the second (*) corresponds to polarized functions. The geometry was optimised to lower the interaction energy between water molecules and carbon quantum dot. Obtained data was visualised using Discovery Studio software package [55]. It was also observed that the theoretical calculations performed are in sound agreement with the obtained experimental results and thus validates our model.

From Fig. 13 (a–c), it is evident that the water molecules interact with carbon quantum dots when aligned at about 3 Å and consequently form hydrogen bonds. It is also evident from the computational experiment that the interaction occurs by forming the layers and thus providing a larger surface area of CQD for better interaction and thus owing to stability of the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrostatic and hydrogen bonding charge densities
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 13 represents the isocontour surfaces for H-donor/acceptor clouded regions at binding sites for (a) one, (b) two and (c) three water molecules with GCQDs. These acceptor and donor regions assist in finding the electron rich and electron deficient sites in the GCQD-Water system and thus helps in the prediction of possible hydrogen binding sites within the system. These regions represent those atoms which have the tendency to donate/accept electrons so as to achieve stability during their geometry optimization. Also, this shows the isocontour surface distribution of electrostatic charges throughout the system leading to the electron transfer between definite pair of atoms; and eventually no distortions in the chemical structures are offered. The values of electrostatic potentials are represented using different colours in the isocontour surface of the GCQD-Water system shown in Fig. 13c. The electrostatic potential increases in the order of following colours; red < orange < yellow < green < blue (however, here we have only red and blue) and is shown in Fig. 13. The blue colour indicates the regions of highest attraction whereas red colour indicates the region corresponding to highest repulsion in the electrostatic isocontour potential maps of GCQD-Water system.

Fig. 14 displays the chemical stability of the GCQD-Water system which can be estimated from the HOMO (Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital) and LUMO (Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital) energy levels. The HOMO-LUMO gap, also known as band gap (in case of continuous bands) was calculated in order to get an atomistic insight of the optoelectronic characteristics regarding the optical activity of GCQD [56,57]. The HOMO–LUMO of the GCQD-Water system are shown in Table 5 .

These energies associated to HOMO and LUMO orbitals, reflect the electron donating and accepting tendencies, respectively as shown in Fig. 15 . Some of the other crucial physical and chemical parameters such as; ionization potential, electron affinity, electronegativity, absolute hardness and softness can also be calculated theoretically, at the same level by using HOMO and LUMO energy differences and thus can add mode to the theoretically calculated databases [58–60].

We have successfully synthesized the gram carbon quantum dots via waste gram peels, which is the source of carbon. As synthesized carbon quantum dot is water soluble and shows the green and blue luminescent property. We have prepared GCQDs at two different temperatures 200 °C (C-1) and 450 °C (C-2) and the average size of GCQDs are 7 and 5 nm, respectively. In UV light samples C-1 and C-2 exhibit green and blue emission respectively. The chemical compostions of GCQDs were confirmed by Raman and XPS analysis. The simple and cost effective GCQDs showed good sensing characteristics. We have used both the samples C-1 and C-2 for the capacitive humidity sensor at different frequencies (20 Hz - 2 MHz). Among C-1 and C-2 based sensors, C-2 displays the best result at optimized frequency. The response and recovery time for sample C-1 are 13.3 s and 14.1 s and for C-2 these are 7.3 s and 4.7 s, respectively. From the theortical study we can conclude that the water molecules easily interact with carbon quantum dots. The electrostatic isocontour map shows that the red regions represent the most electronegative sites, whereas the blue regions represent most electropositve sites within the CQD-water system. These sites also reveal crucial informations concerning the regions where the CQD-water system can undergo intra- and intermolecular interactions to attain stability. Since the energy gap between HOMO and LUMO is very small and this energy gap is sufficient to give significant information about the CQD-water system that it has almost planar geometry and assists in layer based interaction and hence tends to decrease the HOMO-LUMO gap. Thus gram carbon quatum dots easily interact with water molecules and performed as humidity sensor. Also, the theoretical data confirmed the experimental results.

Priyanka Chaudhary: Writing - original draft, Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Resources. Dheeraj Kumar Maurya: Validation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Sarita Yadav: Methodology, Visualization, Data curation, Methodology. Anwesh Pandey: Software. Ravi Kant Tripathi: Methodology, Visualization. B.C. Yadav: Supervision, Conceptualization, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Priyanka Chaudhary acknowledges UGC, New Delhi for non NET fellowship. S. Yadav acknowledges DST for Inspire Faculty research grant (DST/INSPIRE/04/2016/001600). Dr. Ravi Kant Tripathi acknowledges to the Government of India for providing research grant as UGC sponsored Dr. D.S. Kothari Post-doctoral fellowship (No. F.4-2/2006(BSR)/PH/16-17/0054). Authors would also like to thank Prof. Devesh Kumar for Gaussian 09 software package and valuable discussion in theoretical part.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene nanomesh as highly sensitive chemiresistor gas sensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. K. Paul, S. Badhulika, N. M. Saucedo, A. Mulchandani, Graphene nanomesh as highly sensitive chemiresistor gas sensor, Anal. Chem. 84 (2012) 8171−8178.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ms. Priyanka Chaudhary is pursuing Ph.D. in Physics at Babasaheb Bhimrao Ambedkar (Central) University, Lucknow, U.P., India. She has received her B.Sc. and M.Sc., M.Phil. degrees in 2012, 2014 and 2016, respectively from University of Lucknow, Lucknow and BBA (Central) University, Lucknow. Her research area of interest includes the investigations on nanometallopolymers and quantum dots with their humidity and gas sensing applications.

Mr. Dheeraj Kumar Maurya is pursuing Ph.D. in Physics at Centre for Nanoscience & Technology, Pondicherry University, Puducherry, India. He has received his B.Sc., M.Sc. and M.Phil. degrees in 2012, 2014 and 2016, respectively from Bareilly and BBA (Central) University, Lucknow. His research area of interest includes the area of Development of Polymer nanocomposites for sensors and energy storage applications.

Dr. Sarita Yadav has received her Ph.D. degree in Physics in 2014 from Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, India. She is awardee of prestigious INSPIRE Faculty Award-2016 sponsored by Department of Science and Technology, Govt. of India. Presently, she is working as INSPIRE Faculty in Physics Department, University of Allahabad, Prayagraj. Her area of research is organic electronics, in particular, the fabrication of low voltage flexible organic devices and the synthesis of nanocomposites for sensing applications.

Mr. Anwesh Pandey received his B.Sc. degree in 2014 from DDU Gorakhpur University and M.Sc. degree in 2016 from BBA Central University, Lucknow, U.P., India. Currently, he is pursuing his Ph.D. in Computational Biophysics from the same University. His areas of interest are computational simulations of complex biological and chemical systems.

Dr. Ravi Kant Tripathi received his B.Sc. and M.Sc. degree from D. D. U Gorakhpur University, Gorakhpur. Thereafter he received his Ph.D. Degree (2016) from AcSIR-National Physical Laboratory, New Delhi. He is currently a UGC DSK postdoctoral fellow in Nanomaterials and Sensors Research Laboratory, Department of Physics at Babasaheb Bhimrao Ambedkar University, Lucknow, U.P., India. His current research interest includes design, development and application of nanohybrid materials as photosensors and flexible sensors. Dr. Tripathi has published more than Thirty six research papers in different international journals.

Prof. (Dr.) B.C. Yadav was born at Hardatt Nagar Girant, Shravasti, Uttar Pradesh, India. Prof. Yadav has received his Ph.D. degree in 2001 from Department of Physics, University of Lucknow, India. Currently, he is Professor and Head of the Department of Physics, School of Physical & Decision Sciences in the Babasaheb Bhimrao Ambedkar University, Lucknow. Also, Dr. Yadav is Director/Incharge of University Sophisticated Instrumentation Center (USIC). He is the recipient of prestigious Young Scientist Award-2005 instituted by the State Council of Science and Technology, Uttar Pradesh, India. Also, Dr. Yadav was selected in 2011 for Brain Pool International Fellowship of South Korea. He has published more than one hundred ninety six research/review papers in reputed international journals and guided 18 Ph. D. students. National Academy of Sciences, India has nominated to Dr. Yadav as the member. His current interests of research include the synthesis of metal oxides nanoparticles, metallopolymers, nanohybrid materials, carbon nanostructures and their applications as physical and chemical sensors.
